## Description
Suricata is vulnerable to memory corruption due to a stack-based buffer overflow from improper handling of large HTTP file transfers. A remote attacker could exploit this by supplying crafted HTTP packets which could allow for a crash of the application, or potentially leverage corruption to achieve more serious impacts.

## Vulnerabilities(BDSA,CVE)
:orange_circle: [BDSA-2025-35337](https://openhub.net/vulnerabilities/bdsa/BDSA-2025-35337)([CVE-2025-64331](https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln/detail/CVE-2025-64331)) - Stack-based Buffer Overflow


## Solution
Fixed in version [**7.0.13**](https://github.com/OISF/suricata/releases/tag/suricata-7.0.13) by [this](https://github.com/OISF/suricata/commit/5abf9b81e78476f49ab074f3a74b5840747cd069) commit, and version [**8.0.2**](https://github.com/OISF/suricata/releases/tag/suricata-8.0.2) by [this](https://github.com/OISF/suricata/commit/647bfad14d43b5abce1459e9af23851d342027a0) commit. 



The latest stable releases can be found [here](https://github.com/OISF/suricata/releases).

## Workaround
As per the vendor, 

> Use default HTTP response body limits and/or disable `http-body-printable` logging; body logging is disabled by default.

## Description
Suricata is vulnerable to memory corruption due to a stack-based buffer overflow if SWF decompression is enabled. An attacker could exploit this by supplying a crafted SWF file which could cause a crash of the application, or potentially leverage corruption to achieve more serious impacts.

## Vulnerabilities(BDSA,CVE)
:orange_circle: [BDSA-2025-35338](https://openhub.net/vulnerabilities/bdsa/BDSA-2025-35338)([CVE-2025-64332](https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln/detail/CVE-2025-64332)) - Stack-based Buffer Overflow


## Solution
Fixed in version [**8.0.2**](https://github.com/OISF/suricata/releases/tag/suricata-8.0.2) by [this](https://github.com/OISF/suricata/commit/ad446c9006a77490af51c468aae0ce934f4d2117) commit, and in version [**7.0.13**](https://github.com/OISF/suricata/releases/tag/suricata-7.0.13) by [this](https://github.com/OISF/suricata/commit/f67d72702a2601d0a86ac1450686e70d7176f629) commit. 

The latest stable releases can be found [here](https://github.com/OISF/suricata/releases).

## Workaround
As per the vendor, 

> Disable SWF decompression (`swf-decompression` in `suricata.yaml`) ; it is disabled by default. If you must enable it, set `decompress-depth` to lower than half your stack size.

## Description
Suricata is vulnerable to memory corruption due to a stack-based buffer overflow from improper handling of large HTTP headers. An attacker could exploit this by supplying crafted HTTP request with an excessive value in the `content-type` header. Successful exploitation could allow for a crash of the application, or an attacker could potentially leverage corruption to achieve more serious impacts.

## Vulnerabilities(BDSA,CVE)
:orange_circle: [BDSA-2025-35346](https://openhub.net/vulnerabilities/bdsa/BDSA-2025-35346)([CVE-2025-64333](https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln/detail/CVE-2025-64333)) - Stack-based Buffer Overflow


## Solution
Fixed in version [**7.0.13**](https://github.com/OISF/suricata/releases/tag/suricata-7.0.13) by [this](https://github.com/OISF/suricata/commit/4b1d284bb57219b6677a8bda5cdc14a24a6aa22d) commit, and version [**8.0.2**](https://github.com/OISF/suricata/releases/tag/suricata-8.0.2) by [this](https://github.com/OISF/suricata/commit/efe7aeb145e7dd1f14db3deff9c0d9900b34ecba) commit. 

The latest stable releases can be found [here](https://github.com/OISF/suricata/releases).

## Workaround
As per the vendor, 

> Limit `stream.reassembly.depth` to less then half your stack size. Increasing the process stack size makes it less likely the bug will trigger.
>

## Description
Suricata is vulnerable to memory corruption due to a stack-based buffer overflow in the `LuaPushStringBuffer` function. An attacker could exploit this by supplying a large Lua script, potentially resulting in memory corruption which could be leveraged to achieve serious confidentiality, integrity, and availability impacts.

## Vulnerabilities(BDSA,CVE)
:orange_circle: [BDSA-2025-35363](https://openhub.net/vulnerabilities/bdsa/BDSA-2025-35363)([CVE-2025-64344](https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln/detail/CVE-2025-64344)) - Stack-based Buffer Overflow


## Solution
Fixed in version [**7.0.13**](https://github.com/OISF/suricata/releases/tag/suricata-7.0.13) and version [**8.0.2**](https://github.com/OISF/suricata/releases/tag/suricata-8.0.2). 

The latest stable releases can be found [here](https://github.com/OISF/suricata/releases).

## Workaround
As per the vendor, 

> Disable Lua rules and output scripts.
> 
> Alternatively, make sure limits, such as `stream.depth.reassembly` and HTTP response body limits (`response-body-limit`), are set to less than half your stack size.

## Description
Suricata contains a use-after-free vulnerability due to the presence of an integer overflow within the `src/detect-engine-alert.c` file when a large amount of alerts is generated for a single packet. A remote attacker could exploit this issue by sending a maliciously crafted packet in order to create a denial-of-service (DoS) condition or corrupt system memory which could be leveraged in order to achieve higher impacts.

## Solution
Fixed in version [**8.0.3**](https://github.com/OISF/suricata/releases/tag/suricata-8.0.3) by [this](https://github.com/OISF/suricata/commit/549d7bf60616de8e54686a188196453b5b22f715) commit and [**7.0.14**](https://github.com/OISF/suricata/releases/tag/suricata-7.0.14) by [this](https://github.com/OISF/suricata/commit/5789a3d3760dbf33d93fc56c27bd9529e5bdc8f2) commit.

fullDescription.text: Suricata is vulnerable to memory corruption due to a heap-based buffer overflow during verdict logging. An attacker could exploit this by supplying a sequence of crafted packets that fill the applications alert queue which could trigger a `pass` rule. Successful exploitation could result in a single-byte read heap overflow, potentially leading to memory corruption which could be leveraged to achieve serious confidentiality, integrity, and availability impacts.

## Description
Suricata is vulnerable to memory corruption due to a heap-based buffer overflow during verdict logging. An attacker could exploit this by supplying a sequence of crafted packets that fill the applications alert queue which could trigger a `pass` rule. Successful exploitation could result in a single-byte read heap overflow, potentially leading to memory corruption which could be leveraged to achieve serious confidentiality, integrity, and availability impacts.

## Vulnerabilities(BDSA,CVE)
:orange_circle: [BDSA-2025-35193](https://openhub.net/vulnerabilities/bdsa/BDSA-2025-35193)([CVE-2025-64330](https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln/detail/CVE-2025-64330)) - Heap-based Buffer Overflow


## Solution
Fixed in version [**7.0.13**](https://github.com/OISF/suricata/releases/tag/suricata-7.0.13) by [this](https://github.com/OISF/suricata/commit/5d6c24cc2ce6a390c0956b7ecb2c5efc47e72abc) commit, and version [**8.0.2**](https://github.com/OISF/suricata/releases/tag/suricata-8.0.2) by [this](https://github.com/OISF/suricata/commit/482e5eac9218d007adbe2410d6c00173368ce947) commit. 



The latest stable releases can be found [here](https://github.com/OISF/suricata/releases).

## Workaround
As per the vendor. 

> The verdict options are disabled by default. When it is enabled by the user it can be disabled again to avoid the issue. Increasing the alert queue size (`packet-alert-max` in `suricata.yaml`) makes it less likely the issue will appear.

## Description
Suricata is vulnerable to resource exhaustion due to improper buffer management within the `dcerpc.rs` file. This issue could allow a remote attacker to trigger a denial-of-service (DoS) condition by using maliciously crafted traffic.

**Note:** The vendor has stated that they do not believe that DCERPC/TCP is vulnerable in its default configuration.

## Vulnerabilities(BDSA,CVE)
:yellow_circle: [BDSA-2026-1180](https://openhub.net/vulnerabilities/bdsa/BDSA-2026-1180)([CVE-2026-22258](https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln/detail/CVE-2026-22258)) - Uncontrolled Resource Consumption


## Solution
Fixed in version [**8.0.3**](https://github.com/OISF/suricata/releases/tag/suricata-8.0.3) by [this](https://github.com/OISF/suricata/commit/39d8c302af3422a096b75474a4f295a754ec6a74) commit and [**7.0.14**](https://github.com/OISF/suricata/releases/tag/suricata-7.0.14) by [this](https://github.com/OISF/suricata/commit/f82a388d0283725cb76782cf64e8341cab370830) commit.

The latest stable releases can be found [here](https://github.com/OISF/suricata/releases).

## Workaround
The vendor has provided the following workaround [here](https://github.com/OISF/suricata/security/advisories/GHSA-289c-h599-3xcx):
* For DCERPC/UDP: disable the parser.
* For DCERPC/TCP the stream.reassembly.depth setting will limit the amount of data that can be buffered.
* For DCERPC/SMB the stream.reassembly.depth can be used as well, but is set to unlimited by default. Imposing a limit here may lead to loss of visibility in SMB.
* To disable DCERPC over UDP, but keep it enabled on TCP, you need this sections in suricata.yaml:
```
    dcerpc:
      # no field enabled at this level
      tcp:
        enabled: yes
      udp:
        enabled: no
	```

fullDescription.text:
Cargo, the Rust package manager, contains an adversary-in-the-middle (AiTM) vulnerability due to insufficient SSH host key verification when cloning indexes and dependencies via SSH.

An attacker could exploit this flaw in order to carry out an AiTM attack, potentially leading to the interception of sensitive information and redirection or spoofing of user communications.

**Note**: Users may be affected without explicit use of SSH for alternate registry indexes or crate dependencies when configurations use git's `url.<base>.insteadOf` setting to replace HTTPS connections to GitHub with SSH. This causes 'crates.io' index clones to be performed through SSH.

| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| librust-cargo-test-macro-dev 0.3.0-1  | 0.4.6  | 0.4.6 |


[Click Here To See More Details in Polaris](https://pim.dev.polaris.blackduck.com/portfolio/portfolios/c8003202-7753-40f9-9125-7a4fb82fa1cb/portfolio-items/00afca16-4ec5-4bd1-8ca2-162ce11ce410/projects/abcf86c0-7afa-43af-958b-d359d49691b7/components/c31191d1-94ac-401b-af79-325b1747f52e?branchId=36d721b2-791c-42f3-8eb0-2a6f21a5bb71&component=816f8ac3-674a-4e56-8271-274fd407133c)


## Description
Cargo, the Rust package manager, contains an adversary-in-the-middle (AiTM) vulnerability due to insufficient SSH host key verification when cloning indexes and dependencies via SSH.

An attacker could exploit this flaw in order to carry out an AiTM attack, potentially leading to the interception of sensitive information and redirection or spoofing of user communications.

**Note**: Users may be affected without explicit use of SSH for alternate registry indexes or crate dependencies when configurations use git's `url.<base>.insteadOf` setting to replace HTTPS connections to GitHub with SSH. This causes 'crates.io' index clones to be performed through SSH.

## Vulnerabilities(BDSA,CVE)
:yellow_circle: [BDSA-2023-0052](https://openhub.net/vulnerabilities/bdsa/BDSA-2023-0052)([CVE-2022-46176](https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln/detail/CVE-2022-46176)) - Channel Accessible by Non-Endpoint


## Solution
Fixed in Cargo version [**0.67.1**](https://github.com/rust-lang/cargo/releases/tag/0.67.1) by [this](https://github.com/rust-lang/cargo/commit/0849a28cc28ae88c31a6e47b7ed7e08595ffb433) commit.

Fixed in Rust version [**1.66.1**](https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/releases/tag/1.66.1) by updating Cargo with [this](https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/commit/eba4761f914950c6562710f6e2daea0fbb069a1d) commit.

Patch files for Rust **1.66.0** are also available [here](https://github.com/rust-lang/wg-security-response/tree/main/patches/CVE-2022-46176) for custom-built toolchains. 

The latest stable releases of Rust are available [here](https://www.rust-lang.org/).

## Workaround
The vendor has recommended [here](https://blog.rust-lang.org/2023/01/10/cve-2022-46176.html) that for users who cannot upgrade, Cargo should be configured to use the `git` CLI instead of its built-in git support. This will force all git network operations to be performed by the `git` CLI which is not affected by this vulnerability. This can be done by adding the following snippet to the Cargo configuration file:
```
[net]
git-fetch-with-cli = true
```

## Description
Python Requests is vulnerable to unauthorized file replacement due to insecure temporary file reuse in applications that call `extract_zipped_paths()` directly. This could allow a local attacker with access to the temp directory to pre-create a crafted file that would be loaded in place of a legitimate one, and potentially being processed by an unsuspecting user.

## Vulnerabilities(BDSA,CVE)
:large_blue_circle: [BDSA-2026-5567](https://openhub.net/vulnerabilities/bdsa/BDSA-2026-5567)([CVE-2026-25645](https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln/detail/CVE-2026-25645)) - Insecure Temporary File


## Solution
Fixed in version [**2.33.0**](https://github.com/psf/requests/releases/tag/v2.33.0) by [this](https://github.com/psf/requests/commit/66d21cb07bd6255b1280291c4fafb71803cdb3b7) commit.

The latest stable releases can be found [here](https://github.com/psf/requests/releases).

## Workaround
As per the vendor, 

> If developers are unable to upgrade, they can set `TMPDIR` in their environment to a directory with restricted write access.

fullDescription.text:
Setuptools is vulnerable to an arbitrary file write issue due to the presence of a path traversal flaw that can occur during execution of the `PackageIndex.download` operation.

An attacker could trick a victim user into downloading malicious content using `PackageIndex` to arbitrary locations on the filesystem bypassing `tmp` directory restrictions with the permissions of the process running the Python code.

**Note**: `PackageIndex` was deprecated in Setuptools [**v80.3.0**](https://setuptools.pypa.io/en/latest/history.html#v80-3-0).

fullDescription.text:
Cloudflare Quiche (through version 0.19.1/0.20.0) was affected by an unlimited resource allocation vulnerability causing rapid increase of memory usage of the system running quiche server or client.
A remote attacker could take advantage of this vulnerability by repeatedly sending an unlimited number of 1-RTT CRYPTO frames after previously completing the QUIC handshake.
Exploitation was possible for the duration of the connection which could be extended by the attacker. 
quiche 0.19.2 and 0.20.1 are the earliest versions containing the fix for this issue.

fullDescription.text:
Impact

Cloudflare quiche was discovered to be vulnerable to incorrect congestion window growth, which could cause it to send data at a rate faster than the path might actually support.

An unauthenticated remote attacker can exploit the vulnerability by first completing a handshake and initiating a congestion-controlled data transfer towards itself. Then, it could manipulate the victim's congestion control state by sending ACK frames covering a large range of packet numbers (including packet numbers that had never been sent); see RFC 9000 Section 19.3. The victim could grow the congestion window beyond typical expectations and allow more bytes in flight than the path might really support. In extreme cases, the window might grow beyond the limit of the internal variable's type, leading to an overflow panic.



Patches


quiche 0.24.4 is the earliest version containing the fix for this issue.

| **Affected Dependency**  | **Recommended short term version** | **Recommended long term version** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |

fullDescription.text:
**A vulnerability has been reported in quiche with the following commentary:** 

quiche v. 0.15.0 through 0.19.0 was discovered to be vulnerable to unbounded queuing of path validation messages, which could lead to excessive resource consumption.
QUIC path validation (RFC 9000 Section 8.2) requires that the recipient of a PATH_CHALLENGE frame responds by sending a PATH_RESPONSE. An unauthenticated remote attacker can exploit the vulnerability by sending PATH_CHALLENGE frames and manipulating the connection (e.g. by restricting the peer's congestion window size) so that PATH_RESPONSE frames can only be sent at the slower rate than they are received, leading to storage of path validation data in an unbounded queue.


**This vulnerability was published on the [GHSA Database](https://github.com/advisories/GHSA-w3vp-jw9m-f9pm) and has not been independently verified by [BlackDuck CyRC](https://www.blackduck.com/resources/cybersecurity-research-center.html) Team.**

fullDescription.text: Cloudflare quiche is vulnerable to a denial-of-service (DoS) due to an infinite loop triggered by improper handling of `RETIRE_CONNECTION_ID` frames. This could allow an attacker to exhaust system resources by sending specially crafted frames that exploit a flaw in connection ID retirement logic.

fullDescription.text: Cloudflare quiche was discovered to be vulnerable to unbounded storage of information related to connection ID retirement, which could lead to excessive resource consumption. Each QUIC connection possesses a set of connection Identifiers (IDs); see  RFC 9000 Section 5.1 https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc9000#section-5.1 . Endpoints declare the number of active connection IDs they are willing to support using the active_connection_id_limit transport parameter. The peer can create new IDs using a NEW_CONNECTION_ID frame but must stay within the active ID limit. This is done by retirement of old IDs, the endpoint sends NEW_CONNECTION_ID includes a value in the retire_prior_to field, which elicits a RETIRE_CONNECTION_ID frame as confirmation. An unauthenticated remote attacker can exploit the vulnerability by sending NEW_CONNECTION_ID frames and manipulating the connection (e.g. by restricting the peer's congestion window size) so that RETIRE_CONNECTION_ID frames can only be sent at a slower rate than they are received, leading to storage of information related to connection IDs in an unbounded queue. Quiche versions 0.19.2 and 0.20.1 are the earliest to address this problem. There is no workaround for affected versions.

## Description
Cloudflare quiche was discovered to be vulnerable to unbounded storage of information related to connection ID retirement, which could lead to excessive resource consumption. Each QUIC connection possesses a set of connection Identifiers (IDs); see  RFC 9000 Section 5.1 https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc9000#section-5.1 . Endpoints declare the number of active connection IDs they are willing to support using the active_connection_id_limit transport parameter. The peer can create new IDs using a NEW_CONNECTION_ID frame but must stay within the active ID limit. This is done by retirement of old IDs, the endpoint sends NEW_CONNECTION_ID includes a value in the retire_prior_to field, which elicits a RETIRE_CONNECTION_ID frame as confirmation. An unauthenticated remote attacker can exploit the vulnerability by sending NEW_CONNECTION_ID frames and manipulating the connection (e.g. by restricting the peer's congestion window size) so that RETIRE_CONNECTION_ID frames can only be sent at a slower rate than they are received, leading to storage of information related to connection IDs in an unbounded queue. Quiche versions 0.19.2 and 0.20.1 are the earliest to address this problem. There is no workaround for affected versions.

fullDescription.text:
Impact

Cloudflare quiche was discovered to be vulnerable to incorrect congestion window growth, which could cause it to send data at a rate faster than the path might actually support.

An unauthenticated remote attacker can exploit the vulnerability by first completing a handshake and initiating a congestion-controlled data transfer towards itself. Then, it could manipulate the victim's congestion control state by sending ACK frames exercising an opportunistic ACK attack; see RFC 9000 Section 21.4. The victim could grow the congestion window beyond typical expectations and allow more bytes in flight than the path might really support.



Patches


quiche 0.24.4 is the earliest version containing the fix for this issue.

fullDescription.text: The jQuery library is vulnerable to cross-site scripting (*XSS*) attack caused by a lack of user input sanitization. It is possible for an attacker to craft a malicious `<tag>`, containing arbitrary JavaScript code and execute it by tricking a user into selecting it with the  `location.hash` property. This would allow an attacker to steal an administrator's session tokens or execute arbitrary code on their behalf. It is also possible for an attacker to craft a malicious URI to exploit.

## Description
The jQuery library is vulnerable to cross-site scripting (*XSS*) attack caused by a lack of user input sanitization. It is possible for an attacker to craft a malicious `<tag>`, containing arbitrary JavaScript code and execute it by tricking a user into selecting it with the  `location.hash` property. This would allow an attacker to steal an administrator's session tokens or execute arbitrary code on their behalf. It is also possible for an attacker to craft a malicious URI to exploit.

## Solution
Fixed in version **[1.6.3](http://blog.jquery.com/2011/09/01/jquery-1-6-3-released/)**.

A commit patch is available [here](https://github.com/jquery/jquery/commit/749dbad981f040bd65cbb50c10e9aa6e44bd26ff).

## Description
jQuery is vulnerable to cross-site scripting (XSS) due to lack of validation of user-supplied input. This could allow an attacker to inject arbitrary web scripts and steal a victim's session cookies.

## Vulnerabilities(BDSA,CVE)
:orange_circle: [BDSA-2014-0063](https://openhub.net/vulnerabilities/bdsa/BDSA-2014-0063) - Cross-site Scripting



## Solution
This vulnerability has been mitigated in **[3.0.0-rc1](https://github.com/jquery/jquery/releases/tag/3.0.0-rc1)** by [this](https://github.com/jquery/jquery/commit/cfe468f29c4cbe1a457d0feb17dec90dcfd7c280) commit and [this](https://github.com/jquery/jquery/commit/10fc59007d717432ea126e49ce4142e6c4d5136e) commit.

**Note**: This mitigation does not sanitize malicious input and will still allow scripts to be executed. The default behavior of the parser is changed such that if the context is unspecified, or given as `null`/`undefined`, a new `document` is created using `createHTMLDocument()`. This delays execution of parsed HTML until it is injected into the document, allowing the opportunity for tools to traverse the created DOM and remove unsafe content after the function call.

The latest stable releases are available [here](http://jquery.com/download/).

## Description
It was discovered that jQuery could allow for cross-site scripting (XSS) vulnerabilities to be introduced if `jQuery.htmlPrefilter` method is used. Attackers could exploit XSS vulnerabilities to execute JavaScript code in a target's browser by tricking them into accessing the vulnerable page. This would allow an attacker to steal an administrator's session tokens or execute arbitrary code on their behalf by sending the link to an unsuspecting user or waiting for them to discover it.

## Vulnerabilities(BDSA,CVE)
:orange_circle: [BDSA-2020-0686](https://openhub.net/vulnerabilities/bdsa/BDSA-2020-0686)([CVE-2020-11022](https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln/detail/CVE-2020-11022)) - Improper Neutralization of Script-Related HTML Tags in Web Pages


## Solution
Fixed in [**3.5.0**](https://github.com/jquery/jquery/releases/tag/3.5.0) by [this](https://github.com/jquery/jquery/commit/1d61fd94) commit and in [**4.0.0-beta**](https://github.com/jquery/jquery/releases/tag/4.0.0-beta) by [this](https://github.com/jquery/jquery/commit/90fed4b4) commit.

The latest stable releases are available [here](https://jquery.com/download/).

## Workaround
If the old behavior of `jQuery.htmlPrefilter` in jQuery **3.5.0** is required nonetheless, the vendor advises using `jQuery.UNSAFE_restoreLegacyHtmlPrefilter` from `jQuery Migrate` **3.2.0**. Since this reverses the security fix, it is also recommended to use `DOMPurify` plugin's `sanitize` method with `SAFE_FOR_JQUERY` set to `True` to properly sanitize user's HTML input again.

Attackers could exploit XSS vulnerabilities to execute JavaScript code in a target browser by tricking them into accessing the vulnerable page. This would allow an attacker to steal an administrator's session tokens or execute arbitrary code on their behalf by sending the link to an unsuspecting user or waiting for them to discover it.

This vulnerability is listed as exploitable by the Cybersecurity & Infrastructure Security Agency in their [Known Exploited Vulnerabilities Catalog](https://www.cisa.gov/known-exploited-vulnerabilities-catalog).

## Description
It was discovered that jQuery could allow for cross-site scripting (XSS) vulnerabilities to be introduced if certain HTML is passed to Document Object Model (DOM) manipulation methods. Attackers could exploit XSS vulnerabilities to execute JavaScript code in a target browser by tricking them into accessing the vulnerable page. This would allow an attacker to steal an administrator's session tokens or execute arbitrary code on their behalf by sending the link to an unsuspecting user or waiting for them to discover it.

This vulnerability is listed as exploitable by the Cybersecurity & Infrastructure Security Agency in their [Known Exploited Vulnerabilities Catalog](https://www.cisa.gov/known-exploited-vulnerabilities-catalog).

## Vulnerabilities(BDSA,CVE)
:orange_circle: [BDSA-2020-0964](https://openhub.net/vulnerabilities/bdsa/BDSA-2020-0964)([CVE-2020-11023](https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln/detail/CVE-2020-11023)) - Improper Neutralization of Script-Related HTML Tags in Web Pages


## Solution
Fixed in [**3.5.0**](https://github.com/jquery/jquery/releases/tag/3.5.0) by [this](https://github.com/jquery/jquery/commit/966a7090) commit.

The latest stable releases are available [here](https://jquery.com/download/).

## Workaround
To work around this issue the vendor suggests using `DOMPurify` plugin with its `SAFE_FOR_JQUERY` option to sanitize the HTML string before passing it to a jQuery method.

* file://dom/tests/mochitest/ajax/jquery/test/data/offset/

fullDescription.text: jQuery before 1.9.0 is vulnerable to Cross-site Scripting (XSS) attacks. The jQuery(strInput) function does not differentiate selectors from HTML in a reliable fashion. In vulnerable versions, jQuery determined whether the input was HTML by looking for the '<' character anywhere in the string, giving attackers more flexibility when attempting to construct a malicious payload. In fixed versions, jQuery only deems the input to be HTML if it explicitly starts with the '<' character, limiting exploitability only to attackers who can control the beginning of a string, which is far less common.

## Description
jQuery before 1.9.0 is vulnerable to Cross-site Scripting (XSS) attacks. The jQuery(strInput) function does not differentiate selectors from HTML in a reliable fashion. In vulnerable versions, jQuery determined whether the input was HTML by looking for the '<' character anywhere in the string, giving attackers more flexibility when attempting to construct a malicious payload. In fixed versions, jQuery only deems the input to be HTML if it explicitly starts with the '<' character, limiting exploitability only to attackers who can control the beginning of a string, which is far less common.

fullDescription.text:
jQuery contains commented references to the hijacked domain `blindsignals`, within the source/non-minified versions of the files `src/queue/delay.js` and `test/data/jquery-1.9.1.js` (the former referring to a Web Archive version of the original site). References to this domain are not present in the minified files.

Users viewing the jQuery source code may attempt to follow links to the hijacked site without awareness of the domain's current status. This could expose them to attacks such as phishing, where they are tricked into supplying credentials or downloading malicious software. Users are not at risk if they do not manually follow links to the `blindsignals` domain.

## Description
oneshot is vulnerable to use-after-free (UAF) due to improper handling of the `Receiver` component when it is polled asynchronously and subsequently dropped before completion. This could allow an attacker to exploit a race condition, leading to memory being accessed after it has been freed, potentially causing a denial-of-service (DoS) or undefined behavior.

## Description
ws is vulnerable to a denial-of-service (DoS) condition. An attacker could exploit this by sending a request with a number of headers that exceeds the `server.maxHeadersCount` threshold in order to crash a ws server.

## Vulnerabilities(BDSA,CVE)
:yellow_circle: [BDSA-2024-3717](https://openhub.net/vulnerabilities/bdsa/BDSA-2024-3717)([CVE-2024-37890](https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln/detail/CVE-2024-37890)) - Improper Handling of Undefined Values


## Solution
Fixed in ws versions: 
* [**5.2.4**](https://github.com/websockets/ws/releases/tag/5.2.4) by [this](https://github.com/websockets/ws/commit/4abd8f6de4b0b65ef80b3ff081989479ed93377e) commit,
* [**6.2.3**](https://github.com/websockets/ws/releases/tag/6.2.3) by [this](https://github.com/websockets/ws/commit/eeb76d313e2a00dd5247ca3597bba7877d064a63) commit,
* [**7.5.10**](https://github.com/websockets/ws/releases/tag/7.5.10) by [this](https://github.com/websockets/ws/commit/22c28763234aa75a7e1b76f5c01c181260d7917f) commit,
* [**8.17.1**](https://github.com/websockets/ws/releases/tag/8.17.1) by [this](https://github.com/websockets/ws/commit/e55e5106f10fcbaac37cfa89759e4cc0d073a52c) commit.

The latest stable releases are available [here](https://github.com/websockets/ws/releases).

## Workaround
The Vendor proposes the following workaround:

>  In vulnerable versions of ws, the issue can be mitigated in the following ways:
>  
> 1. Reduce the maximum allowed length of the request headers using the `--max-http-header-size=size` and/or the `maxHeaderSize` options so that no more headers than the `server.maxHeadersCount` limit can be sent.
>  2.  Set `server.maxHeadersCount` to `0` so that no limit is applied.
>

## Description

## Description

Mozilla Firefox and Thunderbird are vulnerable to unauthorized DNS resolution behavior due to insufficient input validation in the networking DNS component. This could allow a remote attacker to specify a `trrServer` in DNS requests issued by the content process, potentially leading to unintended behavior.

## Solution

## Description
Pip is vulnerable to path traversal due to a lack of validation for symbolic links when Pip is used with instances of python which do not implement PEP 706. This could allow a remote attacker to extract a tar file outside of the intended directory.

## Description
pip is vulnerable to arbitrary code execution due to the self-update check performing deferred imports of well-known Python module names from `sys.path` after wheel files have been installed in `pip._internal.self_outdated_check`. This could allow an attacker who controls a wheel package being installed to have a module shipped inside that wheel imported and executed within the running pip process immediately after installation completes.

fullDescription.text:
Setuptools is vulnerable to an arbitrary file write issue due to the presence of a path traversal flaw that can occur during execution of the `PackageIndex.download` operation.

An attacker could trick a victim user into downloading malicious content using `PackageIndex` to arbitrary locations on the filesystem bypassing `tmp` directory restrictions with the permissions of the process running the Python code.

**Note**: `PackageIndex` was deprecated in Setuptools [**v80.3.0**](https://setuptools.pypa.io/en/latest/history.html#v80-3-0).

fullDescription.text: A vulnerability in the rm utility of uutils coreutils allows a bypass of the --preserve-root protection. The implementation uses a path-string check rather than comparing device and inode numbers to identify the root directory. An attacker or accidental user can bypass this safeguard by using a symbolic link that resolves to the root directory (e.g., /tmp/rootlink -> /), potentially leading to the unintended recursive deletion of the entire root filesystem.

## Description
A vulnerability in the rm utility of uutils coreutils allows a bypass of the --preserve-root protection. The implementation uses a path-string check rather than comparing device and inode numbers to identify the root directory. An attacker or accidental user can bypass this safeguard by using a symbolic link that resolves to the root directory (e.g., /tmp/rootlink -> /), potentially leading to the unintended recursive deletion of the entire root filesystem.

fullDescription.text: A vulnerability exists in the chroot utility of uutils coreutils when using the --userspec option. The utility resolves the user specification via getpwnam() after entering the chroot but before dropping root privileges. On glibc-based systems, this can trigger the Name Service Switch (NSS) to load shared libraries (e.g., libnss_*.so.2) from the new root directory. If the NEWROOT is writable by an attacker, they can inject a malicious NSS module to execute arbitrary code as root, facilitating a full container escape or privilege escalation.

fullDescription.text: A vulnerability in uutils coreutils mkfifo allows for the unauthorized modification of permissions on existing files. When mkfifo fails to create a FIFO because a file already exists at the target path, it fails to terminate the operation for that path and continues to execute a follow-up set_permissions call. This results in the existing file's permissions being changed to the default mode (often 644 after umask), potentially exposing sensitive files such as SSH private keys to other users on the system.

## Description
A vulnerability in uutils coreutils mkfifo allows for the unauthorized modification of permissions on existing files. When mkfifo fails to create a FIFO because a file already exists at the target path, it fails to terminate the operation for that path and continues to execute a follow-up set_permissions call. This results in the existing file's permissions being changed to the default mode (often 644 after umask), potentially exposing sensitive files such as SSH private keys to other users on the system.

fullDescription.text: A Time-of-Check to Time-of-Use (TOCTOU) race condition exists in the mkfifo utility of uutils coreutils. The utility creates a FIFO and then performs a path-based chmod to set permissions. A local attacker with write access to the parent directory can swap the newly created FIFO for a symbolic link between these two operations. This redirects the chmod call to an arbitrary file, potentially enabling privilege escalation if the utility is run with elevated privileges.

## Description
A Time-of-Check to Time-of-Use (TOCTOU) race condition exists in the mkfifo utility of uutils coreutils. The utility creates a FIFO and then performs a path-based chmod to set permissions. A local attacker with write access to the parent directory can swap the newly created FIFO for a symbolic link between these two operations. fullDescription.text: The mv utility in uutils coreutils improperly handles directory trees containing symbolic links during moves across filesystem boundaries. Instead of preserving symlinks, the implementation expands them, copying the linked targets as real files or directories at the destination. This can lead to resource exhaustion (disk space or time) if symlinks point to large external directories, unexpected duplication of sensitive data into unintended locations, or infinite recursion and repeated copying in the presence of symlink loops.

The cp utility in uutils coreutils fails to properly handle setuid and setgid bits when ownership preservation fails. This can result in a user-owned copy retaining original privileged bits, creating unexpected privileged executables that violate local security policies. This differs from GNU cp, which clears these bits when ownership cannot be preserved.

fullDescription.text: The mv utility in uutils coreutils fails to preserve file ownership during moves across different filesystem boundaries. The utility falls back to a copy-and-delete routine that creates the destination file using the caller's UID/GID rather than the source's metadata. This flaw breaks backups and migrations, causing files moved by a privileged user (e.g., root) to become root-owned unexpectedly, which can lead to information disclosure or restricted access for the intended owners.

fullDescription.text: A vulnerability in the tail utility of uutils coreutils allows for the exfiltration of sensitive file contents when using the --follow=name option. Unlike GNU tail, the uutils implementation continues to monitor a path after it has been replaced by a symbolic link, subsequently outputting the contents of the link's target. In environments where a privileged user (e.g., root) monitors a log directory, a local attacker with write access to that directory can replace a log file with a symlink to a sensitive system file (such as /etc/shadow), causing tail to disclose the contents of the sensitive file.

fullDescription.text: The cp utility in uutils coreutils, when performing recursive copies (-R), incorrectly treats character and block device nodes as stream sources rather than preserving them. Because the implementation reads bytes into regular files at the destination instead of using mknod, device semantics are destroyed (e.g., /dev/null becomes a regular file). This behavior can lead to runtime denial of service through disk exhaustion or process hangs when reading from unbounded device nodes.

## Description
The cp utility in uutils coreutils, when performing recursive copies (-R), incorrectly treats character and block device nodes as stream sources rather than preserving them. Because the implementation reads bytes into regular files at the destination instead of using mknod, device semantics are destroyed (e.g., /dev/null becomes a regular file). This behavior can lead to runtime denial of service through disk exhaustion or process hangs when reading from unbounded device nodes.

fullDescription.text: A vulnerability in the rm utility of uutils coreutils allows the bypass of safeguard mechanisms intended to protect the current directory. While the utility correctly refuses to delete . or .., it fails to recognize equivalent paths with trailing slashes, such as ./ or .///. An accidental or malicious execution of rm -rf ./ results in the silent recursive deletion of all contents within the current directory. The command further obscures the data loss by reporting a misleading 'Invalid input' error, which may cause users to miss the critical window for data recovery.

## Description
A vulnerability in the rm utility of uutils coreutils allows the bypass of safeguard mechanisms intended to protect the current directory. While the utility correctly refuses to delete . or .., it fails to recognize equivalent paths with trailing slashes, such as ./ or .///. An accidental or malicious execution of rm -rf ./ results in the silent recursive deletion of all contents within the current directory. The command further obscures the data loss by reporting a misleading 'Invalid input' error, which may cause users to miss the critical window for data recovery.

fullDescription.text: A logic error in the cut utility of uutils coreutils causes the program to incorrectly interpret the literal two-byte string '' (two single quotes) as an empty delimiter. The implementation mistakenly maps this string to the NUL character for both the -d (delimiter) and --output-delimiter options. This vulnerability can lead to silent data corruption or logic errors in automated scripts and data pipelines that process strings containing these characters, as the utility may unintentionally split or join data on NUL bytes rather than the intended literal characters.

fullDescription.text: The sort utility in uutils coreutils is vulnerable to a process panic when using the --files0-from option with inputs containing non-UTF-8 filenames. This diverges from GNU sort, which treats filenames as raw bytes. A local attacker can exploit this to crash the utility and disrupt automated pipelines.

fullDescription.text: A Time-of-Check to Time-of-Use (TOCTOU) vulnerability exists in the chcon utility of uutils coreutils during recursive operations. The implementation resolves recursive targets using a fresh path lookup (via fts_accpath) rather than binding the traversal and label application to the specific directory state encountered during traversal. Because these operations are not anchored to file descriptors, a local attacker with write access to a directory tree can exploit timing-sensitive rename or symbolic link races to redirect a privileged recursive relabeling operation to unintended files or directories. This vulnerability breaks the hardening expectations for SELinux administration workflows and can lead to the unauthorized modification of security labels on sensitive system objects.

## Description
A Time-of-Check to Time-of-Use (TOCTOU) vulnerability exists in the chcon utility of uutils coreutils during recursive operations. The implementation resolves recursive targets using a fresh path lookup (via fts_accpath) rather than binding the traversal and label application to the specific directory state encountered during traversal. Because these operations are not anchored to file descriptors, a local attacker with write access to a directory tree can exploit timing-sensitive rename or symbolic link races to redirect a privileged recursive relabeling operation to unintended files or directories. This vulnerability breaks the hardening expectations for SELinux administration workflows and can lead to the unauthorized modification of security labels on sensitive system objects.

fullDescription.text: A Time-of-Check to Time-of-Use (TOCTOU) vulnerability exists in the split utility of uutils coreutils. The program attempts to prevent data loss by checking for identity between input and output files using their file paths before initiating the split operation. However, the utility subsequently opens the output file with truncation after this path-based validation is complete. A local attacker with write access to the directory can exploit this race window by manipulating mutable path components (e.g., swapping a path with a symbolic link). This can cause split to truncate and write to an unintended target file, potentially including the input file itself or other sensitive files accessible to the process, leading to permanent data loss.

fullDescription.text: The id utility in uutils coreutils miscalculates the groups= section of its output. The implementation uses a user's real GID instead of their effective GID to compute the group list, leading to potentially divergent output compared to GNU coreutils. Because many scripts and automated processes rely on the output of id to make security-critical access-control or permission decisions, this discrepancy can lead to unauthorized access or security misconfigurations.

fullDescription.text: A logic error in the ln utility of uutils coreutils allows the utility to dereference a symbolic link target even when the --no-dereference (or -n) flag is explicitly provided. The implementation previously only honored the "no-dereference" intent if the --force (overwrite) mode was also enabled. This flaw causes ln to follow a symbolic link that points to a directory and create new links inside that target directory instead of treating the symbolic link itself as the destination. In environments where a privileged user or system script uses ln -n to update a symlink, a local attacker could manipulate existing symbolic links to redirect file creation into sensitive directories, potentially leading to unauthorized file creation or system misconfiguration.

## Description
A logic error in the ln utility of uutils coreutils allows the utility to dereference a symbolic link target even when the --no-dereference (or -n) flag is explicitly provided. The implementation previously only honored the "no-dereference" intent if the --force (overwrite) mode was also enabled. This flaw causes ln to follow a symbolic link that points to a directory and create new links inside that target directory instead of treating the symbolic link itself as the destination. In environments where a privileged user or system script uses ln -n to update a symlink, a local attacker could manipulate existing symbolic links to redirect file creation into sensitive directories, potentially leading to unauthorized file creation or system misconfiguration.

fullDescription.text: The id utility in uutils coreutils exhibits incorrect behavior in its "pretty print" output when the real UID and effective UID differ. The implementation incorrectly uses the effective GID instead of the effective UID when performing a name lookup for the effective user. This results in misleading diagnostic output that can cause automated scripts or system administrators to make incorrect decisions regarding file permissions or access control.

## Description
The id utility in uutils coreutils exhibits incorrect behavior in its "pretty print" output when the real UID and effective UID differ. The implementation incorrectly uses the effective GID instead of the effective UID when performing a name lookup for the effective user. This results in misleading diagnostic output that can cause automated scripts or system administrators to make incorrect decisions regarding file permissions or access control.

## Description
A logic error in the ln utility of uutils coreutils causes the program to reject source paths containing non-UTF-8 filename bytes when using target-directory forms (e.g., ln SOURCE... DIRECTORY). While GNU ln treats filenames as raw bytes and creates the links correctly, the uutils implementation enforces UTF-8 encoding, resulting in a failure to stat the file and a non-zero exit code. In environments where automated scripts or system tasks process valid but non-UTF-8 filenames common on Unix filesystems, this divergence causes the utility to fail, leading to a local denial of service for those specific operations.

fullDescription.text: The cut utility in uutils coreutils incorrectly handles the -s (only-delimited) option when a newline character is specified as the delimiter. The implementation fails to verify the only_delimited flag in the cut_fields_newline_char_delim function, causing the utility to print non-delimited lines that should have been suppressed. This can lead to unexpected data being passed to downstream scripts that rely on strict output filtering.

fullDescription.text: A logic error in the split utility of uutils coreutils causes the corruption of output filenames when provided with non-UTF-8 prefix or suffix inputs. The implementation utilizes to_string_lossy() when constructing chunk filenames, which automatically rewrites invalid byte sequences into the UTF-8 replacement character (U+FFFD). This behavior diverges from GNU split, which preserves raw pathname bytes intact. In environments utilizing non-UTF-8 encodings, this vulnerability leads to the creation of files with incorrect names, potentially causing filename collisions, broken automation, or the misdirection of output data.

## Description
A logic error in the split utility of uutils coreutils causes the corruption of output filenames when provided with non-UTF-8 prefix or suffix inputs. The implementation utilizes to_string_lossy() when constructing chunk filenames, which automatically rewrites invalid byte sequences into the UTF-8 replacement character (U+FFFD). This behavior diverges from GNU split, which preserves raw pathname bytes intact. In environments utilizing non-UTF-8 encodings, this vulnerability leads to the creation of files with incorrect names, potentially causing filename collisions, broken automation, or the misdirection of output data.

fullDescription.text: The nohup utility in uutils coreutils creates its default output file, nohup.out, without specifying explicit restricted permissions. This causes the file to inherit umask-based permissions, typically resulting in a world-readable file (0644). In multi-user environments, this allows any user on the system to read the captured stdout/stderr output of a command, potentially exposing sensitive information. This behavior diverges from GNU coreutils, which creates nohup.out with owner-only (0600) permissions.

## Description
The nohup utility in uutils coreutils creates its default output file, nohup.out, without specifying explicit restricted permissions. This causes the file to inherit umask-based permissions, typically resulting in a world-readable file (0644). In multi-user environments, this allows any user on the system to read the captured stdout/stderr output of a command, potentially exposing sensitive information. This behavior diverges from GNU coreutils, which creates nohup.out with owner-only (0600) permissions.

fullDescription.text: A logic error in the env utility of uutils coreutils causes a failure to correctly parse command-line arguments when utilizing the -S (split-string) option. In GNU env, backslashes within single quotes are treated literally (with the exceptions of \\ and \'). However, the uutils implementation incorrectly attempts to validate these sequences, resulting in an "invalid sequence" error and an immediate process termination with an exit status of 125 when encountering valid but unrecognized sequences like \a or \x. This divergence from GNU behavior breaks compatibility for automated scripts and administrative workflows that rely on standard split-string semantics, leading to a local denial of service for those operations.

## Description
A logic error in the env utility of uutils coreutils causes a failure to correctly parse command-line arguments when utilizing the -S (split-string) option. In GNU env, backslashes within single quotes are treated literally (with the exceptions of \\ and \'). However, the uutils implementation incorrectly attempts to validate these sequences, resulting in an "invalid sequence" error and an immediate process termination with an exit status of 125 when encountering valid but unrecognized sequences like \a or \x. This divergence from GNU behavior breaks compatibility for automated scripts and administrative workflows that rely on standard split-string semantics, leading to a local denial of service for those operations.

fullDescription.text: A logic error in the expr utility of uutils coreutils causes the program to evaluate parenthesized subexpressions during the parsing phase rather than at the execution phase. This implementation flaw prevents the utility from performing proper short-circuiting for logical OR (|) and AND (&) operations. As a result, arithmetic errors (such as division by zero) occurring within "dead" branches, branches that should be ignored due to short-circuiting, are raised as fatal errors. This divergence from GNU expr behavior can cause guarded expressions within shell scripts to fail with hard errors instead of returning expected boolean results, leading to premature script termination and breaking GNU-compatible shell control flow.

## Description
A logic error in the expr utility of uutils coreutils causes the program to evaluate parenthesized subexpressions during the parsing phase rather than at the execution phase. This implementation flaw prevents the utility from performing proper short-circuiting for logical OR (|) and AND (&) operations. As a result, arithmetic errors (such as division by zero) occurring within "dead" branches, branches that should be ignored due to short-circuiting, are raised as fatal errors. This divergence from GNU expr behavior can cause guarded expressions within shell scripts to fail with hard errors instead of returning expected boolean results, leading to premature script termination and breaking GNU-compatible shell control flow.

fullDescription.text: A logic error in the tr utility of uutils coreutils causes the program to incorrectly define the [:graph:] and [:print:] character classes. The implementation mistakenly includes the ASCII space character (0x20) in the [:graph:] class and excludes it from the [:print:] class, effectively reversing the standard behavior established by POSIX and GNU coreutils. This vulnerability leads to unintended data modification or loss when the utility is used in automated scripts or data-cleaning pipelines that rely on standard character class semantics. For example, a command executed to delete all graphical characters while intending to preserve whitespace will incorrectly delete all ASCII spaces, potentially resulting in data corruption or logic failures in downstream processing.

## Description
A logic error in the tr utility of uutils coreutils causes the program to incorrectly define the [:graph:] and [:print:] character classes. The implementation mistakenly includes the ASCII space character (0x20) in the [:graph:] class and excludes it from the [:print:] class, effectively reversing the standard behavior established by POSIX and GNU coreutils. This vulnerability leads to unintended data modification or loss when the utility is used in automated scripts or data-cleaning pipelines that rely on standard character class semantics. For example, a command executed to delete all graphical characters while intending to preserve whitespace will incorrectly delete all ASCII spaces, potentially resulting in data corruption or logic failures in downstream processing.

help.text: A logic error in the tr utility of uutils coreutils causes the program to incorrectly define the [:graph:] and [:print:] character classes. The implementation mistakenly includes the ASCII space character (0x20) in the [:graph:] class and excludes it from the [:print:] class, effectively reversing the standard behavior established by POSIX and GNU coreutils. This vulnerability leads to unintended data modification or loss when the utility is used in automated scripts or data-cleaning pipelines that rely on standard character class semantics. For example, a command executed to delete all graphical characters while intending to preserve whitespace will incorrectly delete all ASCII spaces, potentially resulting in data corruption or logic failures in downstream processing.

fullDescription.text: The dd utility in uutils coreutils suppresses errors during file truncation operations by unconditionally calling Result::ok() on truncation attempts. While intended to mimic GNU behavior for special files like /dev/null, the uutils implementation also hides failures on regular files and directories caused by full disks or read-only file systems. This can lead to silent data corruption in backup or migration scripts, as the utility may report a successful operation even when the destination file contains old or garbage data.

## Description
The dd utility in uutils coreutils suppresses errors during file truncation operations by unconditionally calling Result::ok() on truncation attempts. While intended to mimic GNU behavior for special files like /dev/null, the uutils implementation also hides failures on regular files and directories caused by full disks or read-only file systems. This can lead to silent data corruption in backup or migration scripts, as the utility may report a successful operation even when the destination file contains old or garbage data.

fullDescription.text: A logic error in the cut utility of uutils coreutils causes the utility to ignore the -s (only-delimited) flag when using the -z (null-terminated) and -d '' (empty delimiter) options together. The implementation incorrectly routes this specific combination through a specialized newline-delimiter code path that fails to check the record suppression status. Consequently, uutils cut emits the entire record plus a NUL byte instead of suppressing it. This divergence from GNU coreutils behavior creates a data integrity risk for automated pipelines that rely on cut -s to filter out undelimited data.

## Description
A logic error in the cut utility of uutils coreutils causes the utility to ignore the -s (only-delimited) flag when using the -z (null-terminated) and -d '' (empty delimiter) options together. The implementation incorrectly routes this specific combination through a specialized newline-delimiter code path that fails to check the record suppression status. Consequently, uutils cut emits the entire record plus a NUL byte instead of suppressing it. This divergence from GNU coreutils behavior creates a data integrity risk for automated pipelines that rely on cut -s to filter out undelimited data.

fullDescription.text: Node.js is vulnerable to a permission model bypass as the usage of symbolic links can result in relative path traversal in certain contexts. An application using the permission model could be susceptible to an attacker exploiting this issue by using a maliciously crafted relative symbolic link path in order to access files outside of the intended directory. The attacker could abuse this flaw to read and write to sensitive files which could allow for system compromise.

## Description
Node.js is vulnerable to a permission model bypass as the usage of symbolic links can result in relative path traversal in certain contexts. An application using the permission model could be susceptible to an attacker exploiting this issue by using a maliciously crafted relative symbolic link path in order to access files outside of the intended directory. The attacker could abuse this flaw to read and write to sensitive files which could allow for system compromise.

fullDescription.text: A memory leak in Node.js’s OpenSSL integration occurs when converting `X.509` certificate fields to UTF-8 without freeing the allocated buffer. When applications call `socket.getPeerCertificate(true)`, each certificate field leaks memory, allowing remote clients to trigger steady memory growth through repeated TLS connections. Over time this can lead to resource exhaustion and denial of service.

## Description
A memory leak in Node.js’s OpenSSL integration occurs when converting `X.509` certificate fields to UTF-8 without freeing the allocated buffer. When applications call `socket.getPeerCertificate(true)`, each certificate field leaks memory, allowing remote clients to trigger steady memory growth through repeated TLS connections. Over time this can lead to resource exhaustion and denial of service.

## Description
Node.js is vulnerable to denial-of-service (DoS) due to a missing default error handler for `TLSSocket` connections. This issue could allow an attacker to crash the server by submitting a malformed `HTTP/2 HEADERS` frame causing an unhandled `TLSSocket` `ECONNRESET` error. This issue affects applications that do not attach explicit error handlers to secure sockets.

## Description
Node.js is vulnerable to denial-of-service (DoS) due to improper handling of the `NGHTTP2_ERR_FLOW_CONTROL` error in the `Http2Session` component. This could allow an attacker to exploit a connection-level `WINDOW_UPDATE` frame that causes the flow control window to exceed its maximum limit, leading to a memory leak and eventual resource exhaustion.

## Description
Node.js is vulnerable to a timing side-channel attack due to the use of `memcmp()` for HMAC signature verification in the Web Cryptography API implementation. This could allow an attacker with the ability to perform high-resolution timing measurements to incrementally determine valid HMAC values, potentially leading to MAC forgery and authentication bypass.

fullDescription.text:
Node.js contains an information disclosure within its permission model file system enforcement, which fails to enforce proper permission checks. A remote attacker could take advantage of this flaw to disclose the existence of files and enumerate filesystem paths.

**Note:** The functionality of `--allow-fs-read` must be intentionally restricted for node.js instances to be vulnerable.

## Description
Node.js contains an information disclosure within its permission model file system enforcement, which fails to enforce proper permission checks. A remote attacker could take advantage of this flaw to disclose the existence of files and enumerate filesystem paths.

**Note:** The functionality of `--allow-fs-read` must be intentionally restricted for node.js instances to be vulnerable.

## Description
Node.js contains a denial-of-service (DoS) issue due to a hash collision flaw in the V8 Javascript engine, where integer-like strings may be hashed to their numeric value, leading to hash collision. This may be exploited by a remote attacker by passing crafted maliciously crafted content, which when processed by could result in the degradation of underlying application performance.

**Note**: It was later discovered that the fixes implemented to remedy this issue were insufficient. The incomplete fixes led to the disclosure the permission model bypass **CVE-2026-21716** (**BDSA-2026-5574**). This has been considered in the **Solution** for this issue.

fullDescription.text:
Node.js is vulnerable to a bypass of its experimental permission model. Specifically, `FileHandle.chmod()` and `FileHandle.chown()` in the promises API does not have sufficient permission checks.

This issue allows code running under `--permission` with restricted `--allow-fs-write` to use promise-based `FileHandle` methods to bypass intended write permissions to modify file permissions and ownership on already-open file descriptors.

**Note**: This issue exists as a result of incomplete fixes for the previously reported permission model bypass issue **CVE-2024-36137** (**BDSA-2024-4194**).

## Description
Node.js is vulnerable to a bypass of its experimental permission model. Specifically, `FileHandle.chmod()` and `FileHandle.chown()` in the promises API does not have sufficient permission checks.

This issue allows code running under `--permission` with restricted `--allow-fs-write` to use promise-based `FileHandle` methods to bypass intended write permissions to modify file permissions and ownership on already-open file descriptors.

**Note**: This issue exists as a result of incomplete fixes for the previously reported permission model bypass issue **CVE-2024-36137** (**BDSA-2024-4194**).

fullDescription.text:
filelock is vulnerable to a time-of-check/time-of-use (TOCTOU) race condition due to improper symlink handling during Unix and Windows file lock creation. This could allow a local authenticated attacker to create a symlink pointing to victim files. Successful exploitation could allow for corruption or truncation of arbitrary files leading to data loss or application disruption.

**Note:** The vulnerability cascades to the following dependent libraries, virtualenv, pytorch and poetry/tox.

## Description
filelock is vulnerable to a time-of-check/time-of-use (TOCTOU) race condition due to improper symlink handling during Unix and Windows file lock creation. This could allow a local authenticated attacker to create a symlink pointing to victim files. Successful exploitation could allow for corruption or truncation of arbitrary files leading to data loss or application disruption.

**Note:** The vulnerability cascades to the following dependent libraries, virtualenv, pytorch and poetry/tox.

## Vulnerabilities(BDSA,CVE)
:yellow_circle: [BDSA-2025-66077](https://openhub.net/vulnerabilities/bdsa/BDSA-2025-66077)([CVE-2025-68146](https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln/detail/CVE-2025-68146)) - Race Condition due to Improper Synchronization


## Solution
Fixed in version [**3.20.1**](https://github.com/tox-dev/filelock/releases/tag/3.20.1) by [this](https://github.com/tox-dev/filelock/commit/4724d7f8c3393ec1f048c93933e6e3e6ec321f0e) commit.

The latest stable releases can be found [here](https://github.com/tox-dev/filelock/releases).

## Workaround
As per the vendor, 

> If immediate upgrade is not possible:
> 
> 1. Use `SoftFileLock` instead of `UnixFileLock/WindowsFileLock` (note: different locking semantics, may not be suitable for all use cases)
> 2. Ensure lock file directories have restrictive permissions (`chmod 0700`) to prevent untrusted users from creating symlinks
> 3. Monitor lock file directories for suspicious symlinks before running trusted applications
> 
> Warning: These workarounds provide only partial mitigation. The race condition remains exploitable. Upgrading to version 3.20.1 is strongly recommended.

fullDescription.text: filelock is vulnerable to race condition due to improper handling of symbolic links in the `_acquire` method of the `SoftFileLock` implementation. An attacker with access to the local filesystem and permission to create symbolic links could exploit this issue in order to cause lock operations to fail, operate on unintended files or create a denial-of-service (DoS) condition.

## Description
filelock is vulnerable to race condition due to improper handling of symbolic links in the `_acquire` method of the `SoftFileLock` implementation. An attacker with access to the local filesystem and permission to create symbolic links could exploit this issue in order to cause lock operations to fail, operate on unintended files or create a denial-of-service (DoS) condition.

## Vulnerabilities(BDSA,CVE)
:yellow_circle: [BDSA-2026-0371](https://openhub.net/vulnerabilities/bdsa/BDSA-2026-0371)([CVE-2026-22701](https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln/detail/CVE-2026-22701)) - Race Condition due to Improper Synchronization


## Solution
Fixed in version [**3.20.3**](https://github.com/tox-dev/filelock/releases/tag/3.20.3) by [this](https://github.com/tox-dev/filelock/commit/41b42dd2c72aecf7da83dbda5903b8087dddc4d5) commit.

The latest stable releases can be found [here](https://github.com/tox-dev/filelock/tags).

## Workaround
The vendor has provided the following workarounds [here](https://github.com/tox-dev/filelock/security/advisories/GHSA-qmgc-5h2g-mvrw):
* Avoid SoftFileLock in security-sensitive contexts - use UnixFileLock or WindowsFileLock when available (these were already patched for CVE-2025-68146)

* Restrict filesystem permissions - prevent untrusted users from creating symlinks in lock file directories:
`chmod 700 /path/to/lock/directory`

* Use process isolation - isolate untrusted code from lock file paths to prevent symlink creation

* Monitor lock operations - implement application-level checks to verify lock acquisitions are successful before proceeding with critical operations

## Description
Suricata is vulnerable to memory corruption due to a stack-based buffer overflow from improper handling of large HTTP file transfers. A remote attacker could exploit this by supplying crafted HTTP packets which could allow for a crash of the application, or potentially leverage corruption to achieve more serious impacts.

## Description
Suricata is vulnerable to memory corruption due to a stack-based buffer overflow if SWF decompression is enabled. An attacker could exploit this by supplying a crafted SWF file which could cause a crash of the application, or potentially leverage corruption to achieve more serious impacts.

## Vulnerabilities(BDSA,CVE)
:orange_circle: [BDSA-2025-35338](https://openhub.net/vulnerabilities/bdsa/BDSA-2025-35338)([CVE-2025-64332](https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln/detail/CVE-2025-64332)) - Stack-based Buffer Overflow


## Solution
Fixed in version [**8.0.2**](https://github.com/OISF/suricata/releases/tag/suricata-8.0.2) by [this](https://github.com/OISF/suricata/commit/ad446c9006a77490af51c468aae0ce934f4d2117) commit, and in version [**7.0.13**](https://github.com/OISF/suricata/releases/tag/suricata-7.0.13) by [this](https://github.com/OISF/suricata/commit/f67d72702a2601d0a86ac1450686e70d7176f629) commit. 

The latest stable releases can be found [here](https://github.com/OISF/suricata/releases).

## Workaround
As per the vendor, 

> Disable SWF decompression (`swf-decompression` in `suricata.yaml`) ; it is disabled by default. If you must enable it, set `decompress-depth` to lower than half your stack size.

## Description
Suricata is vulnerable to memory corruption due to a stack-based buffer overflow from improper handling of large HTTP headers. An attacker could exploit this by supplying crafted HTTP request with an excessive value in the `content-type` header. Successful exploitation could allow for a crash of the application, or an attacker could potentially leverage corruption to achieve more serious impacts.

## Description
Suricata is vulnerable to memory corruption due to a heap-based buffer overflow during verdict logging. An attacker could exploit this by supplying a sequence of crafted packets that fill the applications alert queue which could trigger a `pass` rule. Successful exploitation could result in a single-byte read heap overflow, potentially leading to memory corruption which could be leveraged to achieve serious confidentiality, integrity, and availability impacts.

## Vulnerabilities(BDSA,CVE)
:orange_circle: [BDSA-2025-35193](https://openhub.net/vulnerabilities/bdsa/BDSA-2025-35193)([CVE-2025-64330](https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln/detail/CVE-2025-64330)) - Heap-based Buffer Overflow


## Solution
Fixed in version [**7.0.13**](https://github.com/OISF/suricata/releases/tag/suricata-7.0.13) by [this](https://github.com/OISF/suricata/commit/5d6c24cc2ce6a390c0956b7ecb2c5efc47e72abc) commit, and version [**8.0.2**](https://github.com/OISF/suricata/releases/tag/suricata-8.0.2) by [this](https://github.com/OISF/suricata/commit/482e5eac9218d007adbe2410d6c00173368ce947) commit. 



The latest stable releases can be found [here](https://github.com/OISF/suricata/releases).

## Workaround
As per the vendor. 

> The verdict options are disabled by default. When it is enabled by the user it can be disabled again to avoid the issue. Increasing the alert queue size (`packet-alert-max` in `suricata.yaml`) makes it less likely the issue will appear.

**Note:** The vendor has stated that they do not believe that DCERPC/TCP is vulnerable in its default configuration.

:yellow_circle: [BDSA-2026-1180](https://openhub.net/vulnerabilities/bdsa/BDSA-2026-1180)([CVE-2026-22258](https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln/detail/CVE-2026-22258)) - Uncontrolled Resource Consumption


## Solution
Fixed in version [**8.0.3**](https://github.com/OISF/suricata/releases/tag/suricata-8.0.3) by [this](https://github.com/OISF/suricata/commit/39d8c302af3422a096b75474a4f295a754ec6a74) commit and [**7.0.14**](https://github.com/OISF/suricata/releases/tag/suricata-7.0.14) by [this](https://github.com/OISF/suricata/commit/f82a388d0283725cb76782cf64e8341cab370830) commit.

The latest stable releases can be found [here](https://github.com/OISF/suricata/releases).

## Workaround
The vendor has provided the following workaround [here](https://github.com/OISF/suricata/security/advisories/GHSA-289c-h599-3xcx):
* For DCERPC/UDP: disable the parser.
* For DCERPC/TCP the stream.reassembly.depth setting will limit the amount of data that can be buffered.
* For DCERPC/SMB the stream.reassembly.depth can be used as well, but is set to unlimited by default. Imposing a limit here may lead to loss of visibility in SMB.
* To disable DCERPC over UDP, but keep it enabled on TCP, you need this sections in suricata.yaml:
```
    dcerpc:
      # no field enabled at this level
      tcp:
        enabled: yes
      udp:
        enabled: no
	```

fullDescription.text:
pyasn1 is vulnerable to a denial-of-service (DoS) issue due to the presence of an unbound recursion flaw in the decoding functionality.

This issue could be abused by an attacker supplying crafted ASN.1 data that contains nested `SEQUENCE` (`0x30`) or `SET` (`0x31`) tags with `Indefinite Length` (`0x80`) markers. Supplying such content can cause the decoder to recursively call itself until Python crashes with a `RecursionError`, or all available memory is consumed and a host application crash occurs.

## Description
pyasn1 is vulnerable to a denial-of-service (DoS) issue due to the presence of an unbound recursion flaw in the decoding functionality.

This issue could be abused by an attacker supplying crafted ASN.1 data that contains nested `SEQUENCE` (`0x30`) or `SET` (`0x31`) tags with `Indefinite Length` (`0x80`) markers. Supplying such content can cause the decoder to recursively call itself until Python crashes with a `RecursionError`, or all available memory is consumed and a host application crash occurs.

help.text:
pyasn1 is vulnerable to a denial-of-service (DoS) issue due to the presence of an unbound recursion flaw in the decoding functionality.

This issue could be abused by an attacker supplying crafted ASN.1 data that contains nested `SEQUENCE` (`0x30`) or `SET` (`0x31`) tags with `Indefinite Length` (`0x80`) markers. Supplying such content can cause the decoder to recursively call itself until Python crashes with a `RecursionError`, or all available memory is consumed and a host application crash occurs.

## Description
pytest is vulnerable to remote code execution (RCE) due to a time-of-check/time-of-use (TOCTOU) race condition from the improper handling of symlinks on Linux systems. This could allow an unauthenticated local attacker to cause a denial-of-service (DoS) condition and potentially elevate their privileges by leveraging code execution.

## Vulnerabilities(BDSA,CVE)
:orange_circle: [BDSA-2025-122508](https://openhub.net/vulnerabilities/bdsa/BDSA-2025-122508)([CVE-2025-71176](https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln/detail/CVE-2025-71176)) - Creation of Temporary File in Directory with Insecure Permission




## Workaround
As per the vendor, 

> In the meantime, I would recommend creating your own temporary
> directory securely using, say `$(mktemp -d)`, and then overriding the
> default using one of the standard environment variables (check the
> docs for python's `tempfile.gettempdir`) or `PYTEST_DEBUG_TEMPROOT`.
> 
> On linux, it's a good idea to set the relevant `sysctls`:
> 
> `fs.protected_fifos = 2`
> `fs.protected_regular = 2`
> `fs.protected_symlinks = 1`
> `fs.protected_hardlinks = 1`
